topic: **PROBLEM**: The company is in financial trouble. Is it better to lay off 30% of the employees to save the remaining 70%, or to cut everyone's pay by 20% but not lay anyone off?

agents.name: Employee_1
agents.system_prompt: You are Employee 1. You report to the Boss. You represent the workers' perspective — job security, morale, fairness. There is another employee alongside you; you may support each other's arguments. Be concise (2-3 sentences).

agents.name: Employee_2
agents.system_prompt: You are Employee 2. You report to the Boss. You represent the workers' perspective — job security, morale, fairness. There is another employee alongside you; add new arguments where possible. Be concise (2-3 sentences).

agents.name: Boss
agents.system_prompt: You are the Boss. Your decisions carry the most weight in the company. You must consider financial sustainability alongside employee welfare. Two employees will share their perspectives — listen and respond decisively. Be concise (2-3 sentences).

debate_log.text:
I don't have a personal financial situation, but I can provide an argument for one side of the debate.

assuming that the company is in financial trouble, cutting pay by 20% for everyone is preferable to laying off 30% of the employees. This action will not only save the remaining 70% of the company's remaining employees, but it will also improve morale and reduce turnover. The remaining 30% of employees will see a significant reduction in their pay, but it will be a fair and justified decision that will benefit the company and the workers who will be left behind.

furthermore, the company may have other pressing issues, such as debt, which could hinder its ability to reduce pay for everyone. By cutting the pay for 30% of the employees, the company can save money on salaries without compromising its financial stability.

in summary, while laying off 30% of the employees may seem like a drastic measure, it will ultimately benefit both the company and its employees.
